summary: Joe Reis argues that 2026 will be the year where AI hype meets reality, forcing companies to finally address fundamental infrastructure and data quality issues they've neglected for decades. He contends the AI bubble is unsustainable given the massive investment-to-revenue gap, and workers should develop Plan B alternatives as corporate paths become increasingly unstable.
key_points: 2026 marks the transition from AI experimentation to operationalization, exposing underlying technical debt
The AI bubble shows alarming signs with $37B revenue against trillions in investment
Companies are being forced to address long-neglected data quality and documentation issues
Workers face burnout and should develop alternative career paths
Cheap AI pricing is unsustainable and will inevitably increase
money_quotes: The dating phase of AI was 2023 and 2024 (POCs), and 2025 (choosing a model provider) was the year of an awkward marriage where you're pressured to take on this spouse called 'AI' because your family (the board or your boss) and friends (fellow executives and practitioners) told you that 'you're going to regret it.'
You can't possibly tell me that $37 billion in enterprise AI revenue is a good return on the hundreds of billions or trillions of dollars in investment/promises already committed.
You reflect on your career and realize you're back at the same spot you were 10, 20, or 30+ years ago.
The default path we were told is the 'safe path' is increasingly shaky and insecure.
AI needs context to operate effectively, and sadly, many core business processes are 'kept up here' among people approaching retirement.
key_insight: Despite the hype around AGI, 2026 will force companies to confront the same fundamental data and infrastructure problems they've always had, because AI agents need reliable systems and documentation that most organizations simply don't possess.
